coredata.dc:title: Rational design 2D/2D BiOBr/CDs/g-C3N4 Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst with carbon dots as solid-state electron mediators for enhanced visible and NIR photocatalytic activity: Kinetics, intermediates, and mechanism insight
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Mingming
Lai, Cui
Li, Bisheng
Huang, Danlian
Zeng, Guangming
Xu, Piao
Qin, Lei
Liu, Shiyu
Liu, Xigui
Yi, Huan
Li, Minfang
Chu, Chengcheng
Chen, Zhang
coredata.dc:description: Rapid recombination of photogenerated carriers and narrow visible light absorption range are two main defects in graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4)-based photocatalysts. To address these problems, construction of Z-scheme 2D/2D BiOBr/CDs/g-C3N4 heterojunction photocatalysts with carbon dots as solid-state electron mediators has been investigated. The resultant BiOBr/CDs/g-C3N4 hybrids exhibits remarkable interfacial charge transfer abilities and a broadened solar light absorption range owing to the short charge transport distance and the up-converted photoluminescence character of CDs. Simultaneously, the enhanced specific surface area and nanosheet structure impart more active sites to BiOBr/CDs/g-C3N4 composites. As a result, BiOBr/CDs/g-C3N4 composites reveal significant enhancement in the activity of photodegradation of ciprofloxacin (CIP) and tetracycline (TC) under visible and near infrared (NIR) light irradiation. Moreover, the photodegradation efficiency of BiOBr/CDs/g-C3N4 hybrids was significantly enhanced over that of pristine BiOBr nanosheets and g-C3N4 ultrathin nanosheets. The photocatalytic mechanism is expounded according to free radical capture experiments and electron spin resonance spin-trapping tests and the photodegradation intermediates of CIP were detected by liquid chromatography–mass/mass spectrometry. Moreover, BiOBr/CDs/g-C3N4 composites show excellent photostability and reusability after four runs for CIP degradation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalysis
Z-scheme system
Photocatalytic activity
BiOBr/CDs/g-C3N4
Photocatalytic mechanism

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was financially supported by the Program for the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51879101 , 51579098 , 51779090 , 51709101 , 51521006 , 51809090 , 51278176 , 51378190 ), the National Program for Support of Top–Notch Young Professionals of China ( 2014 ), the Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University ( IRT-13R17 ), the Hunan Provincial Science and Technology Plan Project ( 2018SK20410 , 2017SK2243 , 2016RS3026 ), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( 531109200027 , 531107051080 , 531107050978 ), and the Open fund of Hunan Province Key Laboratory of Coal Resources Clean-utilization and Mine Environment Protection ( E21805 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of (a) UCN, (b) CCN, (c) BOR, (d) BCCN-20, (e) BCCN-30, (f) BCCN-40, and (g) BCCN-50.
TEM images of (a) CCN, (b) BCCN-30 composite, (c) HRTEM image of BCCN-30, (d) SEM image of BCCN-30, and (e–i) EDS elements mapping images of BCCN-30 composite.
FT-IR spectra of pure UCN, CCN, and BCCN-30.
(a) Survey XPS spectra and high-resolution (b) C1s, (c) N1s, (d) Br3d, (e) O1s, and (f) Bi4f XPS spectra of BOR, UCN, CCN, and BCCN-30 samples.

(a) The photocatalytic degradation of CIP with as-prepared photocatalyst under visible light, and (b) the pseudo-first-order rate constants of CIP photodegradation over bulk BOR, UCN, CCN, BCCN-20, BCCN-30, BCCN-40, and BCCN-50 composite.
(a) Photocatalytic degradation of TC with as-prepared photocatalyst under NIR light and (b) the relative pseudo-first-order kinetic plots.
Effect of initial (a) Cu2+, (b) Fe3+, (c) NO3 −, and (d) SO4 2− concentrations on the degradation of CIP over BCCN-30 composites.
(a) DRS spectra of as-prepared bulk BOR, UCN, and BCCN composite with different content of BOR; (b) plot of (αhν)2 vs. hν of pure BOR and UCN.
The free radical trapping experiments for degradation of CIP over BCCN-30 composite.

DMPO spin-trapping ESR spectra for BCCN-30 composite (a) in aqueous dispersion for DMPO–
O2 − and (b) in methanol dispersion for DMPO–
OH.
(a) Transient photocurrent density with light on/off cycles and (b) EIS Nyquist plot of the pure BOR, UCN, CCN, and BCCN-30 electrodes measured in 0.5 M Na2SO4.
The proposed photocatalytic mechanism for the degradation of CIP on the surface of BCCN-30 composites.
The proposed photodegradation pathway of CIP by BCCN-30 composite.
Cycling runs of BCCN-30 for (a) CIP and (b) TC degradation under visible light. (c) The XRD pattern of BCCN-30 before and after photocatalytic experiments.

head.ce:title: Rational design 2D/2D BiOBr/CDs/g-C3N4 Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst with carbon dots as solid-state electron mediators for enhanced visible and NIR photocatalytic activity: Kinetics, intermediates, and mechanism insight

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha 410082, Hunan, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Environmental Biology and Pollution Control, Hunan University, Ministry of Education, Changsha 410082, Hunan, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Environmental Biology and Pollution Control

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hunan Province Key Laboratory of Coal Resources Clean-utilization and Mine Environment Protection, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hunan Province Key Laboratory of Coal Resources Clean-utilization and Mine Environment Protection

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha, Hunan 410082, People’s Republic of China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid recombination of photogenerated carriers and narrow visible light absorption range are two main defects in graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4)-based photocatalysts. To address these problems, construction of Z-scheme 2D/2D BiOBr/CDs/g-C3N4 heterojunction photocatalysts with carbon dots as solid-state electron mediators has been investigated. The resultant BiOBr/CDs/g-C3N4 hybrids exhibits remarkable interfacial charge transfer abilities and a broadened solar light absorption range owing to the short charge transport distance and the up-converted photoluminescence character of CDs. Simultaneously, the enhanced specific surface area and nanosheet structure impart more active sites to BiOBr/CDs/g-C3N4 composites. As a result, BiOBr/CDs/g-C3N4 composites reveal significant enhancement in the activity of photodegradation of ciprofloxacin (CIP) and tetracycline (TC) under visible and near infrared (NIR) light irradiation. Moreover, the photodegradation efficiency of BiOBr/CDs/g-C3N4 hybrids was significantly enhanced over that of pristine BiOBr nanosheets and g-C3N4 ultrathin nanosheets. The photocatalytic mechanism is expounded according to free radical capture experiments and electron spin resonance spin-trapping tests and the photodegradation intermediates of CIP were detected by liquid chromatography–mass/mass spectrometry. Moreover, BiOBr/CDs/g-C3N4 composites show excellent photostability and reusability after four runs for CIP degradation.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of medicine, antibiotics have been employed to treat various diseases [1–4]. However, the excessive use of antibiotics causes serious water pollution [3,5–7]. Therefore, it is necessary to find an environmentally friendly technology to remove antibiotics from wastewater [8,9]. Photocatalysis is a hopeful technology for antibiotics removal because of its low cost and freedom from secondary pollution [10,11].

Today, one of the difficult problems is finding suitable photocatalysts for practical application [12]. Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) possesses a narrow band gap (2.7 eV) and an appropriate conduction band (CB) position (−1.07 eV) [13,14]. The photoexcited electron on the CB of g-C3N4 can react with O2 to generate superoxide radicals (
O2 −) and then increase the reactive oxygen species to degrade the pollutant. In addition, g-C3N4 possesses high chemical and thermal stability on account of its tri-s-triazine ring structure and high degree of polymerization [10,15,16]. However, the weak van der Waals interactions between neighboring CN layers in g-C3N4 restrain the photogenerated carrier separation [17]. g-C3N4 also has low surface area and separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers, limiting its practical application [18]. To overcome these problems, various strategies have been used to enhance the photoactivity of g-C3N4, among which are construction of nanostructures [19] and coupling with metals [20] and nonmetallic semiconductors (such as carbon materials or organic materials) [21]. It is noteworthy that the photoactivity can be remarkably enhanced through coupling with nonmetallic semiconductors [22].

Recently, carbon nanomaterials (carbon nanotubes, carbon dots, etc.) have attracted attention on account of their distinct physicochemical characteristics and high aqueous solubility [23–25]. Carbon dots (CDs), with thickness below 10 nm, have attracted more attention due to their photogenerated electron transfer properties and unique optical characteristics [26]. CDs also possess up-converted photoluminescence (PL) characteristics, with a two- or multiphoton reaction process where semiconductors assimilate longer-wavelength light (>700 nm) and emit shorter-wavelength light (<450 nm) [25,27]. CDs can attain full-color light emission under ultraviolet (UV)-to-NIR light irradiation. Therefore, CDs have been successfully coupled with other photocatalysts to improve their catalytic activity by inhibiting photogenerated carrier recombination and broadening the visible light absorption range [28–30]. In addition, the conjugated π structure of CDs can increase the interaction between CDs and semiconductors to form stable composites [31]. Recently, it has been reported that CD-modified g-C3N4 has higher photocatalytic activity for degradation dye, phenol, and water splitting [24,31]. To further improve the quantum efficiency and mineralization ability of CDs/g-C3N4, it is necessary to find suitable semiconductors to couple with CDs/g-C3N4.

Semiconductors can be divided into direct and indirect band gap types. The direct band gap represents a conduction band minimum (CBM) and a valence band maximum (VBM) in the same place in k space, so that the electrons in the VB transfer to the CB to form carriers only needing to absorb energy, leading to fast recombination of photogenerated carriers. However, the indirect band gap makes for the photogenerated electron transfer an inevitable distance in k-space to reach the VB, which reduces the recombination of photogenerated carriers [32]. BiOX (X = Cl, I, Br) have received attention thanks to their indirect transition band gap and 2D layered structure [33,34]. The layered structure can afford enough space to polarize the related atoms and orbitals, leading to the appearance of an internal electric field between the [Bi2O2]2+ layer and the halogen anionic layer in BiOX [35–37]. BiOBr, as one of the bismuth oxyhalides, exhibits high photocatalytic oxidation and reduction activity on account of its narrow band gap (2.81 eV) and more positive valence band position (3.32 eV) than that of g-C3N4 [38–40]. According to previous reports, BiOBr can combine with g-C3N4 to form a traditional heterojunction and shows excellent photocatalytic activity [32]. But it still has the disadvantages of low visible light utilization and mineralization efficiency. To solve these problems, BiOBr can be combined with CDs/g-C3N4 to form a Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst. If this ternary heterojunction is constructed successfully, BiOBr/CDs/g-C3N4 composites can retain the enhanced reduction ability of the g-C3N4 with a more negative CB position and boost the oxidation ability of the BiOBr with a more positive VB position. Moreover, the composites have excellent split efficiency of the photogenerated carriers and a broadened absorption spectrum, thereby enhancing the photoactivity. For all we know, no relevant reports about construction of a 2D–2D Z-scheme BiOBr/CDs/g-C3N4 heterojunction photocatalyst with carbon dots as solid-state electron mediators and research on its photocatalytic properties for degradation of CIP under various conditions.

Here, a novel Z-scheme BiOBr/CDs/g-C3N4 heterojunction photocatalyst was successfully constructed through a facile hydrothermal method. The BiOBr/CDs/g-C3N4 photocatalyst exhibits remarkable photoactivity for degradation of CIP under visible light irradiation. The photodegradation intermediates of CIP were proposed based on liquid chromatography–mass/mass spectrometry. The degradation mechanism of the BiOBr/CDs/g-C3N4 composite for degradation of CIP was revealed by free radical trapping experiments and electron spin resonance (ESR) tests.

Bismuth nitrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), potassium bromide (KBr), nitric acid (HNO3), dicyandiamide, ammonium chloride (NH4Cl), tert-butanol (TB), 4-benzoquinone (BQ), urea, citric acid, sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4), CIP, and TC were purchased from the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (Shanghai, China) and used without further purification.

The CDs were constructed by a hydrothermal method [10]. Briefly, 1 g urea and 3 g citric acid were dissolved into ultrapure water (10 mL) under continuous stirring. The solution was introduced into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and kept at 180 °C for 5 h. After cooling to ambient temperature, the solution was centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 30 min) to get rid of the large particles. Subsequently, the CDs solution was dried at 60 °C to obtain a CD powder.

Ultrathin g-C3N4 (UCN) was synthesized through a facile polymerization method [41]. Dicyandiamide (3 g) and ammonium chloride (15 g) was transferred into 10 mL of ultrapure water. After being mixed mechanically, the solution was completely evaporated at 80 °C. The obtained powder was heated tot 550 °C for 4 h at a heating rate of 3 °C/min. After it cooled to room temperature, a gray powder was separated, repeatedly rinsed with ultrapure water and ethanol, and then dried overnight at 60 °C. In addition, CDs/g-C3N4 (CCN) was prepared by adding CDs to the dicyandiamide and ammonium chloride mixture solution, and other procedures are described above.

BiOBr/CDs/g-C3N4 (BCCN) was constructed by a facile hydrothermal method. In this experiment, Bi(NO3)3·5H2O (2 mmol) was transferred into 18 mL of 1 mol/L HNO3 solution to form solution A, and KBr (2 mmol) was added into 12 mL HNO3 (1 mol/L) solution to form solution B. Subsequently, a certain amount of CCN was introduced into solution B with vigorous stirring at ambient temperature for 60 min. Finally, solution B was transferred into solution A with continued stirring for 30 min. The mixed solution was shifted to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and kept at 160 °C for 6 h. The as-obtained powder was centrifuged, washed, and dried at 60 °C for 24 h. Following the above procedure, different mass ratios of BCCN composites with 20, 30, 40, and 50% BiOBr (abbreviated as BCCN-20, BCCCN-30, BCCN-40, and BCCN-50) were prepared. For comparison, pristine BiOBr (BOR) photocatalysts were prepared under the same conditions without adding the as-synthesized CCN sample.

The crystalline structure of all the prepared photocatalysts was measured by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer) with CuKα radiation. The 2θ range was from 10° to 80° with a 0.04° step at a scanning speed of 8°/min. The morphology of the photocatalyst was testified by transmission electron microscopy (TEM; Tecnai G20, USA) using an acceleration voltage of 200 kV and employing a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi S-4800), and the elemental distributions of photocatalysts were measured through energy-dispersive spectrometry (EDS)–elemental mapping analysis. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Bruker Tensor 27 spectrophotometer. The surface chemical composition and chemical states of photocatalysts were identified via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI-5702, Physical Electronics). The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) was measured on a Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrometer, employing BaSO4 as the reference. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were demonstrated on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan). The photoelectric current (PC) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of the photocatalyst were recorded through a CHI760E A17122 electrochemical workstation. The electron spin resonance (ESR) was measured on a Bruker model ESR JESFA200 spectrometer using spin-trap reagent DMPO in water and methanol.

The photocatalytic performance of all the constructed photocatalysts was measured by degrading CIP and TC under visible and NIR light irradiation. The light source was a 300 W Xe lamp with different cutoff filters. Briefly, 65 mg (20 mg) of synthesized samples was dispersed into 100 mL of 10 mg/L CIP (20 mg/L TC) aqueous solution. Before irradiation, the suspension solution was stirred under dark conduction and maintained for 60 min to reach adsorption–desorption equilibrium. At 15 min (10 min) intervals, about 4 mL of suspension solution was taken out and centrifuged (12,000 rpm, 10 min) to get rid of the photocatalyst. The CIP concentration was detected via the UV–vis spectrophotometer at an absorption wavelength of 276 nm for CIP and 357 nm for TC. The stability of BCCN composites was obtained through four successive cycling experiments.

The photocurrent and EIS were obtained on a CHI760E A17122 electrochemical workstation. In this system, the as-synthesized samples, a platinum foil, an Ag/AgCl electrode, and 0.5 mol/L of Na2SO4 solution served as the working electrode, counter electrode, reference electrode, and electrolyte, respectively. In addition, the visible light source was a 300 W Xe lamp. The working electrode was obtained as follows: Photocatalyst (10 mg) was immersed in 0.4 mL ultrapure water and 0.1 mL of Nafion solution to generate a mixture. Then the mixed solution was dripped onto a 3 × 1 cm fluorinated tin oxide (FTO) glass electrode. The working electrode was calcined at 105 °C for 1 h. All electrochemical experiments were conducted at room temperature.

Fig. 1 exhibits the XRD patterns of all the synthesized photocatalysts. Pure UCN and CCN show similar distinct diffraction peaks at approximately 27.5°, corresponding to the (0 0 2) planes of UCN (JCPDS Card No. 87-1526) [42]. The similar diffraction peaks of UCN and CCN can be put down to the low content of CDs. The typical peaks of pure BOR are in close accord with the tetragonal phase of BiOBr (JCPDS 73-2061) [5]. Moreover, no other characteristic peaks are measured, revealing the high purity and single phase of the synthesized samples. Curves d–g in Fig. 1 show the XRD patterns of composites with different BOR content. The characteristic peak at a 2θ value of 27.5° in the BCCN-20 composite is ascribed to (0 0 2) crystal planes of UCN [41], which indexes that BOR nanoplates are successfully combined with CCN. With the increase of BOR content, the characteristic peak of BOR also increased, while the characteristic peak of UCN decreased. This result shows that the BCCN composite was successfully prepared through a facile hydrothermal method.

The morphology and microstructures of as-constructed photocatalysts were identified via TEM and HRTEM, and the results are exhibited in Fig. 2 and in Fig. S1 in the Supplementary Material. Fig. 2a shows the morphology of as-obtained CCN with irregular nanoplates. It is noteworthy that some nanoparticles adhere on the surfaces of UCN nanosheets and the HRTEM image demonstrates lattice fringes with interplanar spacing of 0.32 nm, corresponding to the (0 0 2) plane of CDs. Fig. 2b exhibits the image of a BCCN-30 composite with two different nanosheets, one of which is a transparent nanosheet, indicating the UCN nanosheet as synthesized. The HRTEM image (Fig. 2c) demonstrates two different lattice fringes with interplanar spacings of 0.28 and 0.32 nm, corresponding to the (1 0 2) plane of BOR and the (0 0 2) plane of CDs, respectively [10]. Thus, the different lattice fringes, the high crystallinity, and the structure of the composite prove successful preparation of BCCN composites.

The SEM image and EDS mapping images of the obtained BCCN-30 composite are displayed in Fig. 2d–i. As shown in Fig. 2d, the nanosheets with different transparency adhere together. From the TEM image of BCCN-30, the transparent nanosheet belongs to CCN, and another nanosheet is BOR. To prove its authenticity, EDS mapping analysis was employed. Fig. 2e–i shows the EDS mapping of C, N, Br, O, and Bi which proves the successful preparation of BCCN hybrid composite.

The surface chemical composition of the as-synthesized photocatalyst was identified by FT-IR. From Fig. 3 , the peaks of pure UCN are located at 1200–1650 cm−1, corresponding to the representative stretching vibration modes of the UCN heterocycles. The characteristic peak at 804 cm−1 is ascribed to the vibration characteristic of 3-s-triazine ring units [43,44]. Notably, those peaks in the CCN composite display a slight red shift from those for pure UCN, revealing that the bond strength of CN was weakened because of the strong force between UCN and CDs. For BCCN-30 composite, the peak at 517 cm−1 is deemed to be the symmetric stretching vibration peak of the BiO band in the BOR structure [45]. The stretching vibration modes of the UCN heterocycles exhibit slight blue shifts, demonstrating that BOR was coupled with UCN heterocycles.

To further demonstrate the chemical compositions and states of synthesized samples, the XPS spectrum was measured. The survey XPS spectrum in Fig. 4 a reveals that the five elements C, N, Br, O, and Bi exist in BCCN-30 composite, and only peaks of Br, O, and Bi exist in pure BOR. For pure UCN, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s at 284.71 and 288.29 eV, which are related to CC bonds and tertiary carbon C(N)3 [46]. Fig. 4c exhibits that the N1s XPS spectrum may be separated into three peaks at 398.71, 400.62, and 404.33 eV, which can be attributed to the N atoms sp 2 hybridized to two C atoms (CNC), the tertiary nitrogen (N(C)3), and graphitic species [47,48]. Compared with pure UCN and CCN, the 404.33 eV of BCCN-30 composites can be found to disappear. This testifies that the coupling of BOR covers up the graphitic species and combines with CCN through an attractive force using π-electrons of CN heterocycles, rather than bonding with C or N atoms of CCN [32]. As for pure BOR (Fig. 4d), the peaks located at 68.40 and 69.43 eV in the XPS spectra of Br3d, corresponding to Br3d 5/2 and Br3d 3/2, indicating that the main chemical states of Br elements were Br1− [5]. The O1s peaks of pure BOR (Fig. 4e) may be separated into two peaks at 529.92 and 531.43 eV, which are related to crystal lattice O atoms (BiO) and surface OH and H2O, respectively [32]. From Fig. 4f, the Bi4f XPS spectrum has peaks centered at 159.35 and 164.53 eV, corresponding to Bi4f 5/2 and Bi4f 7/2, respectively [49]. The finding indicates that the valance state of the Bi element is Bi3+. Therefore, the FT-IR and XPS results strongly testify to the successful preparation of BCCN composite.

The photoactivity of all the constructed photocatalysts was measured by degrading CIP and TC under visible and NIR light. Before irradiation, the adsorption–desorption equilibrium between CIP solution and samples is reached by stirring for 30 min under dark conditions. Fig. 5 a displays that the concentration of CIP has slight change without a photocatalyst, which indicates that the direct photolysis of CIP can be ignored. Pure BOR and UCN possess relatively low photocatalyst activity; only 35.8% and 37.2% of CIP was degraded within 105 min, respectively. The photoactivity of CCN is higher than that of pristine BOR and UCN. To further restrain the recombination efficiency of photocatalytic carriers, BOR was coupled with CCN to form a Z-scheme heterojunction. BCCN-30 has optimal photocatalytic efficiency for degradation of CIP, which was about 2.33 and 2.25 times higher than for pure BOR and UCN, respectively. This phenomenon can be ascribed to the large amount of BOR covering the active sites of CCN and hindering its light absorption, resulting in low photocatalytic performance.

To pursue deep research into the reaction kinetics of photodegradation of CIP by as-synthesized photocatalyst, the experimental data are applied to a pseudo-first-order model,
(1)
ln
C 0
C t
= kt ,
where C 0 and Ct are the concentrations of the contaminants at times 0 and t, respectively, and k is the pseudo-first-order rate constant (min−1) [6]. As exhibited in Fig. 5b, the k value of BCCN-30 was 0.01797 min−1, which is 4.96, 3.90, 2.73, 1.69, 1.45, and 1.45 times higher than for pure BOR, UCN, CCN, BCCN-20, BCCN-40, and BCCN-50. This result further demonstrates that BOR coupled with CCN can enhance the transfer efficiency of photogenerated carriers, and thus increase the photoactivity.

To shield against specific degradation of materials in the photocatalytic process, TC was also employed as a target pollutant. From Fig. S3, 26.3% of TC can be degraded within 60 min by pristine BOR and 41.4% of TC can be removed by pristine UCN under the same conditions. Not surprisingly, BCCN-30 possesses the highest photodegradation efficiency, and the removal efficiency can reach 82.7% under visible light irradiation. The photodegradation process is combined with the pseudo-first order model. The reaction rate of BCCN-30 is 5.70, 3.67, and 1.77 times that of pure BOR, UCN, and CCN, respectively. The above conclusion proves that BCCN-30 composite has a great potential to remove antibiotics from waste water.

In addition, the photocatalytic performance of as-prepared bulk BOR, UCN, CCN and BCCN-30 has been further investigated under NIR light. From Fig. 6 a, the photodegradation efficiency of pure BOR and UCN can be neglected, owing to the low photon energy of NIR light. However, the photocatalytic activity of CCN was improved compared with that of pristine UCN because of the up-converted photoluminescent character of CDs. Moreover, the resultant BCCN-30 hybrid has the highest photodegradation efficiency, and 17.5% of TC was degraded within 180 min. As exhibited in Fig. 6b, the rate constant of BCCN-30 is 31.92, 9.76, and 1.95 times that of pristine BOR, UCN, and CCN, respectively. These results exhibit that the resultant BCCN-30 hybrid offers high photocatalytic performance under both visible and NIR light.

Considering application in pollution wastewater treatment, the photocatalyst activity of pristine BOR, UCN, CCN, and BCCN-30 was measured by degrading CIP under solar light, and the results are exhibited in Fig. S4. For pristine BOR and UCN, 48.3% and 41.5% of CIP is degraded within 60 min, respectively. As was expected, BCCN-30 shows excellent photocatalytic activity and can degrade 81.3% of CIP. Compared with simulated visible light, the photocatalyst exhibits superior photocatalytic activity because solar light contains about 5% ultraviolet light. Hence, the BCCN-30 composite displays enormous potential in waste water treatment.

As is well known, the actual waste water contains many inorganic ions, and those inorganic ions play important roles in photocatalytic system. Hence, Cu2+ and Fe3+ were selected for a study of the detailed impacts on the photocatalytic degradation of CIP by BCCN-30 composite. Fig. 7 a exhibits that the degradation efficiency of CIP is restrained when Cu2+ is added into the CIP solution, and with the increase of Cu2+ concentration, the impact of inhibition gradually increases. This result can be explained by Cu2+ trapping photogenerated electrons that turn Cu2+ into Cu+ and Cu. Moreover, the generated Cu+ and Cu can consume the photogenerated holes to form Cu2+. However, the photogenerated carriers play a main important role in the photocatalytic process, and the electrons and holes are consumed continuously, leading to strong inhibition of CIP degradation [50]. Identically, the effect of Fe3+ with different concentrations (0.005–0.5 mmol/L) on degradation efficiency is researched, and the results are exhibited in Fig. 7b. It can be demonstrated that when the dosage of Fe3+ is lower than 0.100 mmol/L, the removal efficiency does decrease insignificantly, while when the concentration of Fe3+ reaches 0.100 mmol/L, the degradation efficiency decreases to 48%. This is because Fe3+ can be converted into Fe(OH)2+, which possesses strong absorption of solar light when the dosage of Fe3+ is greater than 0.01 mmol/L. Thus, the light quanta received by BCCN-30 decrease and the degradation efficiency will be reduced [51]. In addition, Fe3+ can turn into Fe2+ and Fe4+ by consuming the photogenerated carriers, and thus influence the photocatalytic activity [52].

The effect of NO3 − on the degradation of CIP is displayed in Fig. 7c. It can be observed that both high and low concentrations of NO3 − can inhibit the degradation of CIP, and the high concentration slightly accelerates the photocatalytic activity in the first 15 min. This result can be ascribed to the photolysis of NO3 − forming
OH, and the
OH having strong oxidation capacity for CIP degradation. But if too many NO3 − ions are added, they can adhere to the composite to compete for the active sites on the catalyst surface with CIP. And NO3 − can consume photogenerated electrons, resulting in inhibition of the photodegradation process [53]. As displayed in Fig. 7d, SO4 2− has an insignificant effect on CIP removal under visible light irradiation. This phenomenon is caused by SO4 2− adsorbing onto the surface of BCCN-30 composite and occupying the active sites [50].

The optical properties of pure BOR, UCN, CCN, and BCCN composite with different BOR content were researched by UV–vis DRS, and the results are shown in Fig. 8 . From Fig. 8a, the photoabsorption ranges of bulk BOR and UCN are approximately 500 and 460 nm, respectively. After coupling with CDs, the photoabsorption range of CCN has red-shifted to 700 nm, which indicates that the CDs are possibly settled onto the surface of UCN and thereby alter its optical properties. The visible light absorption ability of CCN composite can be improved because of the inherent optical properties of the CDs. CDs can assimilate longer wavelengths of solar light (≥550 nm) and then excite photoluminescence at a shorter wavelength (400–500 nm) because of their up-converted PL property [31]. However, the optical absorption range of BCCN composite is smaller than that of CCN, which reveals that BOR coupled with CCN does not broaden the light absorption range, but can enhance the separate efficiency of photogenerated carriers. The band gap of semiconductor photocatalysts is obtained from the equation
(2)
α h ν = A
( h ν -
E g
)
n /2
,
where α, h, ν, E g, and A represent the absorption coefficient, Planck constant, light frequency, band gap energy, and a constant, and n is 1 and 4 for the direct and indirect band gap semiconductors, respectively. The n value for pure UCN and BOR is 4, owing to their indirect band-gap semiconductors [5,54]. According to the (αhν)2 against (hν) curve (Fig. 8b), the band gap (E g) of bulk UCN and BOR is 2.64 and 2.81 eV, respectively. The VB and CB potentials for UCN and BOR can be estimated according to the equations
(3)
E
CB
= X -
E e
-
E g
/2,
(4)
E VB
=
E CB
+
E g
,
where E VB is the VB potential, E CB is the CB potential, X is the electronegativity of the semiconductor, and E e is the energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale (about 4.5 eV) [1,22]. The CB and VB potentials of BOR are about +0.54 and +3.35 eV on the basis of the above equations. According to the VB-XPS (Fig. S2d), the VB potential of UCN is approximately +1.57 eV; thus the CB potential is −1.07 eV.

To expound the photocatalytic mechanism of the BCCN-30 photocatalyst, the main active species formed in the photocatalyst system were detected via an active-species-trapping experiment. In this experiment, Na2C2O4, BQ, and TB served as the scavengers for h+,
O2 −, and
OH, respectively. Fig. 9 exhibits the influence of the three scavengers on the photodegradation process. When Na2C2O4, BQ, and TB was added to the photocatalytic process, the photoactivity of BCCN-30 was inhibited and restrained rates of 43.3%, 18.7%, and 12.8%, respectively. This result suggests that h+,
O2 −, and
OH play main roles in the removal of CIP.

To further detect the main active species in this photodegradation system, the ESR spin-trap technique was also implemented. In the ESR test, 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) usually acts as an active special scavenger, generating the stable free radicals DMPO-
O2 − or DMPO-
OH. As exhibited in Fig. 10 , the ESR signal of BCCN-30 cannot be measured under dark conditions. Nevertheless, the characteristic peaks of DMPO-
O2 − can be tested under visible light irradiation. Four strong peaks of DMPO-
OH are measured in aqueous solution. It is noteworthy that the signal intensity of DMPO-
OH increases more than that of DMPO-
O2 − from 5 to 10 min. Hence,
OH plays a more important role in the photodegradation process, which corresponds to that recorded in free radical trapping experiments.

The migration and transfer processes of the photogenerated electron–hole pairs in the photocatalyst were investigated through PL spectra (Fig. S5). Remarkably, the PL spectra of pristine BOR and UCN display a typical peak at about 450 nm. Compared with that of bulk UCN, the PL spectrum of CCN composite reveals a remarkable decrease, which indicates that the doping of CDs can facilitate the migration and transfer of photoinduced electron–hole pairs. Furthermore, the peak at 450 nm of BCCN-30 composite is significantly decreased from that for CCN, revealing that the separation efficiency of photoexcited electrons is improved on account of the generation of Z-scheme BCCN heterojunction photocatalysts.

According to the free radical trapping experiments and photoelectrochemical analysis, a possible photocatalytic mechanism of BCCN-30 photocatalysis for degradation of CIP is elaborated in Fig. 12. Under visible light irradiation, the BOR and UCN are stimulated, and the photoexcited electrons and holes are located in their CB and VB, respectively. CDs serves as the charge transmission bridge in the middle of BOR and UCN [55]. Hence, the excited e− on the CB of BOR and the photogenerated h+ on the VB of UCN are transferred to CDs, leading to photogenerated e− concentrated on the CB of UCN and h+ accumulated on the VB of BOR. Moreover, the intimate contact between BOR and UCN with matched band edge positions, CDs as the charge transmission bridge, and keeping the enhanced reduction ability of the excited e− and the enhanced oxidation ability of the photogenerated h+ to promote higher photocatalytic performance for degradation of CIP is speculated to be the reason for the observed excellent photocatalytic performance of the BCCN photocatalyst. Owing to the CB potential of UCN being more negative than the reduction potential of E° (O2/
O2 −) and the VB potential of BOR being more positive than the potential of E° (
OH/H2O), the photoexcited e− on the CB of UCN can react with O2 to generate
O2 −, and holes can be consumed to produce
OH. As a result, h+,
O2 −, and
OH will react with CIP molecules to form intermediates or directly mineralize into CO2 and H2O in this Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst. The above result is the same as the conclusion of active special capture experiments and ESR tests.

To further research the degradation intermediates, the oxidation products generated during the photocatalytic process were measured by (LC–MS)/MS. The mass spectra, molecular formulas, and chemical structure of the corresponding products are provided in Table S2. In this photocatalyst system, the presence of five products with m/z 306, 291, 263, 245, and 334 indicates that the piperazine ring of CIP is split. According to the experiment data of (LC–MS)/MS, the possible photodegradation pathway was exhibited in Fig. 13 . In pathway 1, the piperazine ring of the CIP molecule is split to generate product A; then product A starts turning into B by cleavage of C2H5N. The presence of product C is produced by the defluorination, and then followed by losing a group of C3H4 to form product D. Simultaneously, product C is converted into E via splitting of CO, releasing NH2 and COOH. In addition, the piperazine ring of the CIP molecule is split by active species to form product F and followed by decarbonylation, which results in the formation of G and H. Subsequently, product H is turned into I by N-dealkylation. Then product J is generated by release of CHON and defluorination. With the process of photodegradation, some intermediates will ultimately be mineralized into H2O and CO2.

The photostability and reusability of BCCN-30 composite was measured via the photodegradation of CIP and TC under visible light irradiation. Before the next cycles, the photocatalysts were centrifuged and washed with deionized water. Then the retrieved catalyst was dried at 60 °C for 24 h. From Fig. 14 a and b, 79.5% of CIP and 76.9% of TC is degraded after 10 degradation–regeneration runs, respectively. Hence, BCCN-30 composite has excellent photostability. To further prove the stability of BCCN-30 hybrid photocatalysts, the XRD pattern of the used photocatalyst was measured. The result (Fig. 14c) shows that the crystal structure does not change, indicating that the as-prepared BCCN-30 composite possesses superior stability and recyclability.

In general, carbon dots acting as solid-state electron mediators for Z-scheme BiOBr/CDs/g-C3N4 hybrid composites were successfully constructed through a hydrothermal method and the photoactivity was researched through degrading CIP and TC under visible and NIR light. BCCN-30 has the highest photodegradation efficiency among pure BOR, UCN, and CCN and BCCN composite with different content of BOR. In this Z-scheme system, the intimate synergistic interactions, the effective photogenerated carrier segregation, the large surface area and enhanced oxidation ability of h+, and the higher reduction capacity of e− lead to remarkable photocatalytic activity. The free radical trapping experiments and ESR test manifest that h+,
OH, and
O2 − are the main active species for degradation of CIP. The possible photodegradation mechanism of BCCN-30 composite to degradation CIP is elaborated. Moreover, the proposed BCCN-30 hybrid composite reveals excellent photostability and reusability after 10 runs under the same conditions. This study is anticipated to open new horizons in the development of photocatalytic technology and designing efficient photocatalysts for practical environmental application.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financially supported by the Program for the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51879101, 51579098, 51779090, 51709101, 51521006, 51809090, 51278176, 51378190), the National Program for Support of Top–Notch Young Professionals of China (2014), the Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University (IRT-13R17), the Hunan Provincial Science and Technology Plan Project (2018SK20410, 2017SK2243, 2016RS3026), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (531109200027, 531107051080, 531107050978), and the Open fund of Hunan Province Key Laboratory of Coal Resources Clean-utilization and Mine Environment Protection (E21805).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcat.2018.11.029.
